content.generation_notice: Fresh live v2 psychology arm. Generator=anthropic; judge=openai. The psychology treatment is a PR #29 sensitivity test, not registry admission.
content.public_question: Should Hungary keep its 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks?
content.summary:
Hungary's 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks sort students at ages 10 and 12, well before the OECD-typical tracking age of 14-16, producing one of the highest levels of academic selectivity (85% of schools select academically, versus a 43% OECD average) and between-school segregation in Europe, alongside a 170-point reading gap between advantaged and disadvantaged deciles. Six directions for decision-makers span a spectrum from inaction to full structural change, and none is a clear winner once empirical uncertainty is separated from underlying value choices about acceptable inequality.

At one pole, maintaining the status quo with added monitoring requires no new legal authority and avoids triggering the church-maintained-school and middle-class veto constituencies that have historically blocked reform. Its constraint is that monitoring is not itself a disciplinary mechanism: Hungary already has assessment infrastructure (ABC testing) that has not historically converted into policy action, so this path risks being read as avoidance rather than diagnosis, especially given the Minister's own acknowledgment of significant social selection and family burden—an admission that turns continued inaction into a value-laden choice, not a neutral default.

At the other pole, delaying selection to age 14 (eliminating the age-10 and age-12 entry points) has the strongest cross-national evidentiary anchor: Poland's 1999 delay from age 15 to 16 reduced between-school variance and produced measured achievement, employment, and earnings gains, especially for previously lower-tracked students. The leading mechanism is closing the multi-stage selection funnel that allows socioeconomic status to determine track placement at increasingly early ages. But Poland's later 2017 reversal—requiring roughly 7,000 school closures and displacing up to 10,000 teachers—illustrates that structural reforms of this scale carry severe reversibility and transition risk, compounded in Hungary by teacher shortages, an aging rural workforce, and a legal inability to bind church and private maintainers, who sit outside central governance authority. Politically, this is the highest-conflict option: even a narrower administrative review was reframed by opposition as an imposed
content.title: Early academic selection and the 6/8-year gimnázium
